assertion.text: Erdős Problem #1108: declared status 'open'. Formalized: yes. Let\[A = \left\{ \sum_{n\in S}n! : S\subset \mathbb{N}\textrm{ finite}\right\}.\]If $k\geq 2$, then does $A$ contain only finitely many $k$th powers? Does it contain only finitely many powerful numbers? Current best: This was motivated in part by a problem of Mahler which he discussed with Erd\H{o}s a few days before his death in 1988: if $k\geq 5$ and\[A_k= \left\{ \sum_{n\in S}k^n : S\subset \mathbb{N}\textrm{ finite}\right\}\]then does $A_k$ contain only finitely many squares? Mahler showed that there are infinitely many squares in $A_k$ for $k\leq 4$, and found only one square for $k\geq 5$, namely\[1+7+7^2+7^3=400.\]Brindza and Erd\H{o}s \cite{BrEr91} proved that, for any $r$, if $n_1!+\cdots+n_r!$ is powerful then $n_1\ll_r 1$. Prize: no. OEIS: A025494, A051761, A115645. Tags: factorials, number theory.
